introSection: Loch Ness Monster is a steel roller coaster located at Busch Gardens Williamsburg in Williamsburg, Virginia, USA. The ride was built by Arrow Development and opened on June 2, 1978. It is the first of two full-circuit roller coasters featuring interlocking loops, with the other being the now defunct Orient Express at Worlds of Fun in Missouri.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
On July 27, 1977, Loch Ness Monster was announced. Construction began that December with a groundbreaking ceremony. The coaster was topped off on April 13, 1978.

Loch Ness Monster opened to the public on June 2, 1978. At that time, it was one of the tallest and fastest roller coasters. The ride costed $5 million to build.

When the coaster first opened, it had four trains with six cars. These trains would go through the interlocking loops at the same time. As the years went on, Loch Ness Monster would go through several changes. The trains would be downsized from four to three with seven cars.

The ride's tunnel was enhanced in 1979 with new special effects.

After the closure of Busch Gardens Tampa's Python in 2006, the trains were sent to Busch Gardens Williamsburg to be reused as spare parts for Loch Ness Monster.

On October 2, 2023, Busch Gardens announced the refurbishment of Loch Ness Monster. As part of the work, of track was replaced and the tunnel was repainted. An advertising campaign was run, including a branded McDonald's meal in the local area. The ride reopened on May 10, 2024.

sections.title: Ride experience
sections.content: The coaster starts with two turns before the lift hill. The train makes a right turn and drops at 60 mph. After the left turn, riders go straight into the first vertical loop, which is high. The train hits the brakes and enters the tunnel with a helix. After exiting the tunnel, the train climbs the second lift hill. It turns right and drops, reaching speeds of 40 mph entering the second vertical loop, which is high. The train then returns to the loading station.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Loch Ness Monster has yellow track and gray supports.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: Before 2018, the ride had trains from Arrow Development. For the ride's 40th anniversary, they were replaced with new trains from S&S Worldwide.

fullText:
Loch Ness Monster is a steel roller coaster located at Busch Gardens Williamsburg in Williamsburg, Virginia, USA. The ride was built by Arrow Development and opened on June 2, 1978. It is the first of two full-circuit roller coasters featuring interlocking loops, with the other being the now defunct Orient Express at Worlds of Fun in Missouri.

On July 27, 1977, Loch Ness Monster was announced. Construction began that December with a groundbreaking ceremony. The coaster was topped off on April 13, 1978.

Loch Ness Monster opened to the public on June 2, 1978. At that time, it was one of the tallest and fastest roller coasters. The ride costed $5 million to build.

When the coaster first opened, it had four trains with six cars. These trains would go through the interlocking loops at the same time. As the years went on, Loch Ness Monster would go through several changes. The trains would be downsized from four to three with seven cars.

The ride's tunnel was enhanced in 1979 with new special effects.

After the closure of Busch Gardens Tampa's Python in 2006, the trains were sent to Busch Gardens Williamsburg to be reused as spare parts for Loch Ness Monster.

On October 2, 2023, Busch Gardens announced the refurbishment of Loch Ness Monster. As part of the work, of track was replaced and the tunnel was repainted. An advertising campaign was run, including a branded McDonald's meal in the local area. The ride reopened on May 10, 2024.



The coaster starts with two turns before the lift hill. The train makes a right turn and drops at 60 mph. After the left turn, riders go straight into the first vertical loop, which is high. The train hits the brakes and enters the tunnel with a helix. After exiting the tunnel, the train climbs the second lift hill. It turns right and drops, reaching speeds of 40 mph entering the second vertical loop, which is high. The train then returns to the loading station.

Loch Ness Monster has yellow track and gray supports.

Before 2018, the ride had trains from Arrow Development. For the ride's 40th anniversary, they were replaced with new trains from S&S Worldwide.
